revision.text: Founded in February 718 (Yoro 2), it is listed as one of 51 of the 122 shrines in Yamashiro Province listed in the Engishiki Shinmeicho (Divine Register of Ancient and Modern Times). (The Shinmeicho lists shrines as "Otsukini Niiname" in the list; shrines that receive offerings at the monthly festival; shrines that receive offerings at the Niiname Festival.) The main deity is Otoshi-no-Okami, with Ishitsukuri-no-Okami and Toyotama-hime-no-Mikoto enshrined in the adjacent hall. The shrine is dedicated to the gods of agriculture, rain, production, and protection from evil. The grounds are known as Kaya-no-Mori (Kaya Forest), and are filled with kaya trees, making it a beloved shrine for Kaya Daimyojin. Designated a Kyoto City Historic Site in 1995, oil is extracted from the nuts of the kaya trees and used as a sacred lamp. Ki no Tsurayuki (editor of the Kokin Wakashū) wrote about the shrine as "Kane no Mori" (Kane no Mori). Regarding the name Otoshi, Motoori Norinaga wrote in his Kojikiden (1798) that "O is a name, and Toshi means rice paddy. By shortening rice paddy, Toshi is read. This deity cultivates rice fields with his spirit, and from those fields, he offers grain to the Emperor. " The co-enshrined deity, Ishitsukuri-no-Okami, is the ancestor of a powerful clan that has made stone coffins for generations. It is said that when Emperor Suinin's Empress, Hibasuhime, passed away, he presented her with a stone coffin and was given the title of "Ishitsukuri-no-Muraji-no-Ko." The year of the joint enshrinement is unknown, but according to oral tradition of the shrine family, it was during the late Edo period. In 1974, the deity was separated into two shrines, Ishikodanchi Shrine in Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture. The current main shrine (one-bay, Nagare-zukuri style) was built in 1690 and relocated from Nagaoka Tenmangu Shrine (Nagaokakyo City) in 1941. A ceremony to enshrine the shrine was held that same year. The original main hall was built in 1680, but is said to have fallen into disrepair. In 2018, it was designated a provisional registered cultural property of Kyoto Prefecture. The Kagura Hall was destroyed by Typhoon Jane in 1950, but was rebuilt in 2013 thanks to the generosity of parishioners. The parishioner festival is held every year on the third Sunday of October, and the head of the Kongo school performs the dedication dances of "Okina" and "Kayanomori" as has been done since the mid-Edo period. In this area, it is said that "without the visit of Okina-san, the rice will not grow." Currently, parishioners are working hard to restore the Kayanomori.

Founded in February 718 (Yoro 2), it is listed as one of 51 of the 122 shrines in Yamashiro Province listed in the Engishiki Shinmeicho (Divine Register of Ancient and Modern Times). (The Shinmeicho lists shrines as "Otsukini Niiname" in the list; shrines that receive offerings at the monthly festival; shrines that receive offerings at the Niiname Festival.) The main deity is Otoshi-no-Okami, with Ishitsukuri-no-Okami and Toyotama-hime-no-Mikoto enshrined in the adjacent hall. The shrine is dedicated to the gods of agriculture, rain, production, and protection from evil. The grounds are known as Kaya-no-Mori (Kaya Forest), and are filled with kaya trees, making it a beloved shrine for Kaya Daimyojin. Designated a Kyoto City Historic Site in 1995, oil is extracted from the nuts of the kaya trees and used as a sacred lamp. Ki no Tsurayuki (editor of the Kokin Wakashū) wrote about the shrine as "Kane no Mori" (Kane no Mori). Regarding the name Otoshi, Motoori Norinaga wrote in his Kojikiden (1798) that "O is a name, and Toshi means rice paddy. By shortening rice paddy, Toshi is read. This deity cultivates rice fields with his spirit, and from those fields, he offers grain to the Emperor. " The co-enshrined deity, Ishitsukuri-no-Okami, is the ancestor of a powerful clan that has made stone coffins for generations. It is said that when Emperor Suinin's Empress, Hibasuhime, passed away, he presented her with a stone coffin and was given the title of "Ishitsukuri-no-Muraji-no-Ko." The year of the joint enshrinement is unknown, but according to oral tradition of the shrine family, it was during the late Edo period. In 1974, the deity was separated into two shrines, Ishikodanchi Shrine in Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture. The current main shrine (one-bay, Nagare-zukuri style) was built in 1690 and relocated from Nagaoka Tenmangu Shrine (Nagaokakyo City) in 1941. A ceremony to enshrine the shrine was held that same year. The original main hall was built in 1680, but is said to have fallen into disrepair. In 2018, it was designated a provisional registered cultural property of Kyoto Prefecture. The Kagura Hall was destroyed by Typhoon Jane in 1950, but was rebuilt in 2013 thanks to the generosity of parishioners. The parishioner festival is held every year on the third Sunday of October, and the head of the Kongo school performs the dedication dances of "Okina" and "Kayanomori" as has been done since the mid-Edo period. In this area, it is said that "without the visit of Okina-san, the rice will not grow." Currently, parishioners are working hard to restore the Kayanomori.

Ki no Tsurayuki (editor of the Kokin Wakashū) wrote about the shrine as "Kane no Mori" (Kane no Mori). Regarding the name Otoshi, Motoori Norinaga wrote in his Kojikiden (1798) that "O is a name, and Toshi means rice paddy. By shortening rice paddy, Toshi is read. This deity cultivates rice fields with his spirit, and from those fields, he offers grain to the Emperor. " The co-enshrined deity, Ishitsukuri-no-Okami, is the ancestor of a powerful clan that has made stone coffins for generations. It is said that when Emperor Suinin's Empress, Hibasuhime, passed away, he presented her with a stone coffin and was given the title of "Ishitsukuri-no-Muraji-no-Ko." The year of the joint enshrinement is unknown, but according to oral tradition of the shrine family, it was during the late Edo period. In 1974, the deity was separated into two shrines, Ishikodanchi Shrine in Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture. The current main shrine (one-bay, Nagare-zukuri style) was built in 1690 and relocated from Nagaoka Tenmangu Shrine (Nagaokakyo City) in 1941. A ceremony to enshrine the shrine was held that same year. The original main hall was built in 1680, but is said to have fallen into disrepair. In 2018, it was designated a provisional registered cultural property of Kyoto Prefecture. The Kagura Hall was destroyed by Typhoon Jane in 1950, but was rebuilt in 2013 thanks to the generosity of parishioners. The parishioner festival is held every year on the third Sunday of October, and the head of the Kongo school performs the dedication dances of "Okina" and "Kayanomori" as has been done since the mid-Edo period. In this area, it is said that "without the visit of Okina-san, the rice will not grow." Currently, parishioners are working hard to restore the Kayanomori.

[[en:Ohotoshino Shrine]] [[Category:Missing wikidata]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:no valid interwikis]]

Founded in February 718 (Yoro 2), it is listed as one of 51 of the 122 shrines in Yamashiro Province listed in the Engishiki Shinmeicho (Divine Register of Ancient and Modern Times). (The Shinmeicho lists shrines as "Otsukini Niiname" in the list; shrines that receive offerings at the monthly festival; shrines that receive offerings at the Niiname Festival.) The main deity is Otoshi-no-Okami, with Ishitsukuri-no-Okami and Toyotama-hime-no-Mikoto enshrined in the adjacent hall. The shrine is dedicated to the gods of agriculture, rain, production, and protection from evil. The grounds are known as Kaya-no-Mori (Kaya Forest), and are filled with kaya trees, making it a beloved shrine for Kaya Daimyojin. Designated a Kyoto City Historic Site in 1995, oil is extracted from the nuts of the kaya trees and used as a sacred lamp. Ki no Tsurayuki (editor of the Kokin Wakashū) wrote about the shrine as "Kane no Mori" (Kane no Mori). Regarding the name Otoshi, Motoori Norinaga wrote in his Kojikiden (1798) that "O is a name, and Toshi means rice paddy. By shortening rice paddy, Toshi is read. This deity cultivates rice fields with his spirit, and from those fields, he offers grain to the Emperor. " The co-enshrined deity, Ishitsukuri-no-Okami, is the ancestor of a powerful clan that has made stone coffins for generations. It is said that when Emperor Suinin's Empress, Hibasuhime, passed away, he presented her with a stone coffin and was given the title of "Ishitsukuri-no-Muraji-no-Ko." The year of the joint enshrinement is unknown, but according to oral tradition of the shrine family, it was during the late Edo period. In 1974, the deity was separated into two shrines, Ishikodanchi Shrine in Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture. The current main shrine (one-bay, Nagare-zukuri style) was built in 1690 and relocated from Nagaoka Tenmangu Shrine (Nagaokakyo City) in 1941. A ceremony to enshrine the shrine was held that same year. The original main hall was built in 1680, but is said to have fallen into disrepair. In 2018, it was designated a provisional registered cultural property of Kyoto Prefecture. The Kagura Hall was destroyed by Typhoon Jane in 1950, but was rebuilt in 2013 thanks to the generosity of parishioners. The parishioner festival is held every year on the third Sunday of October, and the head of the Kongo school performs the dedication dances of "Okina" and "Kayanomori" as has been done since the mid-Edo period. In this area, it is said that "without the visit of Okina-san, the rice will not grow." Currently, parishioners are working hard to restore the Kayanomori.

'''Ōtoshi Shrine (Ōharano-haigata-chō, Kyoto)''' is a {{nihongo|Shikinaisha|式內社}} or a shrine in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|zh|延喜式神名帳|ja|延喜式神名帳}}.
{| class="wikitable sortable" |{{ill|Ōtoshi Shrine (Ōharano-haigata-chō, Kyoto)|WD=Q135038745}} |} Founded in February 718 (Yoro 2), it is listed as one of 51 of the 122 shrines in Yamashiro Province listed in the Engishiki Shinmeicho (Divine Register of Ancient and Modern Times). (The Shinmeicho lists shrines as "Otsukini Niiname" in the list; shrines that receive offerings at the monthly festival; shrines that receive offerings at the Niiname Festival.) The main deity is Otoshi-no-Okami, with Ishitsukuri-no-Okami and Toyotama-hime-no-Mikoto enshrined in the adjacent hall. The shrine is dedicated to the gods of agriculture, rain, production, and protection from evil. The grounds are known as Kaya-no-Mori (Kaya Forest), and are filled with kaya trees, making it a beloved shrine for Kaya Daimyojin. Designated a Kyoto City Historic Site in 1995, oil is extracted from the nuts of the kaya trees and used as a sacred lamp. Ki no Tsurayuki (editor of the Kokin Wakashū) wrote about the shrine as "Kane no Mori" (Kane no Mori). Regarding the name Otoshi, Motoori Norinaga wrote in his Kojikiden (1798) that "O is a name, and Toshi means rice paddy. By shortening rice paddy, Toshi is read. This deity cultivates rice fields with his spirit, and from those fields, he offers grain to the Emperor. " The co-enshrined deity, Ishitsukuri-no-Okami, is the ancestor of a powerful clan that has made Sarcophaguss for generations. It is said that when Emperor Suinin's Empress, Hibasuhime, passed away, he presented her with a Sarcophagus and was given the title of "Ishitsukuri-no-Muraji-no-Ko." The year of the joint enshrinement is unknown, but according to oral tradition of the shrine family, it was during the late Edo period. In 1974, the deity was separated into two shrines, Ishikodanchi Shrine in Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture. The current main shrine (one-bay, Nagare-zukuri style) was built in 1690 and relocated from Nagaoka Tenmangu Shrine (Nagaokakyo City) in 1941. A ceremony to enshrine the shrine was held that same year. The original main hall was built in 1680, but is said to have fallen into disrepair. In 2018, it was designated a provisional registered cultural property of Kyoto Prefecture. The Kagura Hall was destroyed by Typhoon Jane in 1950, but was rebuilt in 2013 thanks to the generosity of parishioners. The parishioner festival is held every year on the third Sunday of October, and the head of the Kongo school performs the dedication dances of "Okina" and "Kayanomori" as has been done since the mid-Edo period. In this area, it is said that "without the visit of Okina-san, the rice will not grow." Currently, parishioners are working hard to restore the Kayanomori. [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamashiro Province]] {{wikidata link|Q135038745}}

In 1974, the deity was separated into two shrines, Ishikodanchi Shrine in Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture. The current main shrine (one-bay, Nagare-zukuri style) was built in 1690 and relocated from Nagaoka Tenmangu Shrine (Nagaokakyo City) in 1941. A ceremony to enshrine the shrine was held that same year. The original main hall was built in 1680, but is said to have fallen into disrepair. In 2018, it was designated a provisional registered cultural property of Kyoto Prefecture. The Kagura Hall was destroyed by Typhoon Jane in 1950, but was rebuilt in 2013 thanks to the generosity of parishioners. The parishioner festival is held every year on the third Sunday of October, and the head of the Kongo school performs the dedication dances of "Okina" and "Kayanomori" as has been done since the mid-Edo period. In this area, it is said that "without the visit of Okina-san, the rice will not grow." Currently, parishioners are working hard to restore the Kayanomori. Designated a Kyoto City Historic Site in 1995, oil is extracted from the nuts of the kaya trees and used as a sacred lamp. Ki no Tsurayuki (editor of the Kokin Wakashū) wrote about the shrine as "Kane no Mori" (Kane no Mori). Regarding the name Otoshi, Motoori Norinaga wrote in his Kojikiden (1798) that "O is a name, and Toshi means rice paddy. By shortening rice paddy, Toshi is read. This deity cultivates rice fields with his spirit, and from those fields, he offers grain to the Emperor. " The co-enshrined deity, Ishitsukuri-no-Okami, is the ancestor of a powerful clan that has made Sarcophaguss for generations. It is said that when Emperor Suinin's Empress, Hibasuhime, passed away, he presented her with a Sarcophagus and was given the title of "Ishitsukuri-no-Muraji-no-Ko." The year of the joint enshrinement is unknown, but according to oral tradition of the shrine family, it was during the late Edo period. In 1974, the deity was separated into two shrines, Ishikodanchi Shrine in Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture. The current main shrine (one-bay, Nagare-zukuri style) was built in 1690 and relocated from Nagaoka Tenmangu Shrine (Nagaokakyo City) in 1941. A ceremony to enshrine the shrine was held that same year. The original main hall was built in 1680, but is said to have fallen into disrepair. In 2018, it was designated a provisional registered cultural property of Kyoto Prefecture. The Kagura Hall was destroyed by Typhoon Jane in 1950, but was rebuilt in 2013 thanks to the generosity of parishioners. The parishioner festival is held every year on the third Sunday of October, and the head of the Kongo school performs the dedication dances of "Okina" and "Kayanomori" as has been done since the mid-Edo period. In this area, it is said that "without the visit of Okina-san, the rice will not grow." Currently, parishioners are working hard to restore the Kayanomori.

In 2018, it was designated a provisional registered cultural property of Kyoto Prefecture. The Kagura Hall was destroyed by Typhoon Jane in 1950, but was rebuilt in 2013 thanks to the generosity of parishioners. The parishioner festival is held every year on the third Sunday of October, and the head of the Kongo school performs the dedication dances of "Okina" and "Kayanomori" as has been done since the mid-Edo period. In this area, it is said that "without the visit of Okina-san, the rice will not grow." Currently, parishioners are working hard to restore the Kayanomori.

revision.text:
'''Ōtoshi Shrine''' is a {{nihongo|Shikinaisha|式內社}} or a shrine in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|zh|延喜式神名帳|ja|延喜式神名帳}}.<ref>https://jmapps.ne.jp/kokugakuin/det.html?data_id=180579</ref>
{| class="wikitable sortable" |{{ill|Ōtoshi Shrine (Ōharano-haigata-chō, Kyoto)|WD=Q135038745|1=Ōtoshi Shrine (Ōharano-haigata-chō, Kyoto)}} |} It was founded in February 718 (Yoro 2), it is listed as one of 51 of the 122 shrines in Yamashiro Province listed in the Engishiki Shinmeicho (Divine Register of Ancient and Modern Times). (The Shinmeicho lists shrines as "Otsukini Niiname" in the list; shrines that receive offerings at the monthly festival; shrines that receive offerings at the Niiname Festival.) The main deity is Otoshi-no-Okami, with Ishitsukuri-no-Okami and Toyotama-hime-no-Mikoto enshrined in the adjacent hall. The shrine is dedicated to the gods of agriculture, rain, production, and protection from evil. The grounds are known as Kaya-no-Mori (Kaya Forest), and are filled with kaya trees, making it a beloved shrine for Kaya Daimyojin. Designated a Kyoto City Historic Site in 1995, oil is extracted from the nuts of the kaya trees and used as a sacred lamp. Ki no Tsurayuki (editor of the Kokin Wakashū) wrote about the shrine as "Kane no Mori" (Kane no Mori). Regarding the name Otoshi, Motoori Norinaga wrote in his Kojikiden (1798) that "O is a name, and Toshi means rice paddy. By shortening rice paddy, Toshi is read. This deity cultivates rice fields with his spirit, and from those fields, he offers grain to the Emperor. " The co-enshrined deity, Ishitsukuri-no-Okami, is the ancestor of a powerful clan that has made Sarcophaguss for generations. It is said that when Emperor Suinin's Empress, Hibasuhime, passed away, he presented her with a Sarcophagus and was given the title of "Ishitsukuri-no-Muraji-no-Ko." The year of the joint enshrinement is unknown, but according to oral tradition of the shrine family, it was during the late Edo period. In 1974, the deity was separated into two shrines, Ishikodanchi Shrine in Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture. The current main shrine (one-bay, Nagare-zukuri style) was built in 1690 and relocated from Nagaoka Tenmangu Shrine (Nagaokakyo City) in 1941. A ceremony to enshrine the shrine was held that same year. The original main hall was built in 1680, but is said to have fallen into disrepair. In 2018, it was designated a provisional registered cultural property of Kyoto Prefecture. The Kagura Hall was destroyed by Typhoon Jane in 1950, but was rebuilt in 2013 thanks to the generosity of parishioners. The parishioner festival is held every year on the third Sunday of October, and the head of the Kongo school performs the dedication dances of "Okina" and "Kayanomori" as has been done since the mid-Edo period. In this area, it is said that "without the visit of Okina-san, the rice will not grow." Currently, parishioners are working hard to restore the Kayanomori.
